approach.reasoning: The agent used the appropriate tool (the tariffs API) and targeted the correct sector (Commercial) and geography (Virginia, with specific cities), which aligns with the suggested approach of using OpenEI tariff data. However, the agent's trace shows only three unfiltered tariff queries and no subsequent filtering or analysis for the specific population of interest (customers with demand >500 kW). The steps do not demonstrate extracting or isolating tariffs that apply to >500 kW customers, nor do they show examining tariff language for demand charge applicability or thresholds. In short: correct data source and reasonable initial queries, but notable gaps in applying the critical filter/analysis (demand >500 kW) and in documenting how tariff entries were selected and interpreted.

accuracy.reasoning: I compared each expected attribute to the agent's answer. Attributes 1–3 expect specific base demand charge numeric values; the agent provided different base numbers for GS-3, GS-4, and Schedule 10 (the agent instead shows higher base rates and different component structure), so those are marked not found. Attributes 4 and 5 are qualitative: the agent explicitly states which schedules apply to customers >500 kW and clearly notes rider/adjustment qualifiers, so those match. Alignment score is matched_attributes/total_attributes = 2/5 = 0.4.

sources.reasoning: The agent cites authoritative primary tariff documents for Dominion (PDF links) which, if real, are appropriate sources for tariff rates; however the answer fails to (a) explicitly reference the required OpenEI IURDB/tariffs database as the source of its data (the question specified OpenEI as the data base), and (b) provide verifiable OpenEI or utility tariff links for Appalachian Power and several claimed schedules. Specific issues: many numeric values are given with high precision and rider adjustments but only some items link to underlying tariff PDFs; Appalachian Power entries lack citation; the claim that Schedule 10 is “effective 2025” and some rider magnitudes and ratchet rules are stated without corresponding OpenEI or utility docket references. Because the evaluation target was explicit inclusion and relevance of sources, this answer is partially acceptable—it uses plausible authoritative sources for Dominion but omits or fails to cite the canonical OpenEI tariff records and supporting utility documents for all asserted rates and adjustments. To be acceptable the agent should (1) cite the exact OpenEI IURDB/tariff entries (or equivalent utility tariff PDFs uploaded to OpenEI) for each listed schedule, (2) provide Appalachian Power tariff references, and (3) indicate the effective dates and rider names/docket numbers for adjustments so the numbers can be verified. Without those, the authority and verifiability of many specific numeric claims are limited.

The steps do not demonstrate extracting or isolating tariffs that apply to >500 kW customers, nor do they show examining tariff language for demand charge applicability or thresholds. In short: correct data source and reasonable initial queries, but notable gaps in applying the critical filter/analysis (demand >500 kW) and in documenting how tariff entries were selected and interpreted.

attribute_details.name: GS-3 (Large General Service Secondary) demand charges
attribute_details.expected: Two demand charge components: $2.498 /kW-month (plus adjustments) and $13.235 /kW-month (plus adjustments); applies for customers with peak >500 kW
attribute_details.agent_value: Dominion GS-3 listed base demand components: Off-Peak $3.636/kW and On-Peak $14.569/kW, plus rider adjustment +$10.39/kW (agent reports totals ~ $14.03/kW off-peak and ~$24.96/kW on-peak)

attribute_details.name: GS-4 (Large General Service Primary/Transmission) demand charges
attribute_details.expected: Two demand charge components: $2.318 /kW-month (plus adjustments) and $12.943 /kW-month (plus adjustments); applies for customers with peak >500 kW
attribute_details.agent_value: Dominion GS-4 listed base demand components: Off-Peak $3.275/kW (≤5,000 kW) and $2.634/kW (>5,000 kW); On-Peak $14.524/kW (≤5,000 kW) and $13.883/kW (>5,000 kW), plus rider adjustment +$9.823/kW (agent reports totals ~ $24.35/kW and ~$23.71/kW depending on size)

attribute_details.name: Schedule 10 / Large General Service (secondary) demand charge
attribute_details.expected: Flat demand component of $2.507 /kW-month (plus adjustments) for secondary-voltage Large General Service
attribute_details.agent_value: Agent lists Schedule 10 (secondary) base flat demand $4.41/kW plus rider +$3.189/kW (agent reports total flat demand ≈ $7.60/kW)

attribute_details.name: Service class coverage for customers >500 kW
attribute_details.expected: Response explicitly states that GS-3 and GS-4 (and Schedule 10 where referenced) apply to commercial customers with peak demand greater than 500 kW
attribute_details.agent_value: Agent explicitly states GS-3 and GS-4 apply for peak demand ≥ 500 kW (and references Schedule 10 eligibility similarly)

attribute_details.name: Notation of adjustments/qualifiers
attribute_details.expected: Answer notes that listed dollar rates are base demand charges and that additional adjustments/charges apply (e.g., riders, taxes, transmission/secondary adjustments) rather than presenting the numbers as all-inclusive final bills
attribute_details.agent_value: Agent repeatedly denotes rider adjustments and notes totals include riders; explicitly cautions that rider adjustments and other charges can significantly increase base demand rates
